items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: How do you say 'Currently, I am using social media' in French?
items.why: Students avoid actuellement thinking it means 'actually'. It really means CURRENTLY. Useful for tech/social media topics. To say 'actually' (in reality), use 'en fait' or 'en réalité'.

items.q: What does 'mes parents' mean in French?
items.correct: My parents (mother and father specifically) - but 'parents' can also mean relatives more broadly.
items.misconception: It always means all my relatives.
items.why: Students think French 'parents' = English 'relatives' because in some contexts it does. Usually 'mes parents' = mother+father; 'mes proches' or 'ma famille' = relatives more broadly. Context matters.

items.q: Which French daily newspaper is considered centre-left?
items.why: It's Le Monde. “Le Figaro” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does 'le quatrieme pouvoir' refer to?
items.why: It's The press as fourth estate. “The military” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was France's foundational press freedom law passed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1881. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
